data.title: Nvidia Brings Groq Tech, Talent In-House
data.description:
NVIDIA is stepping more directly into the fast-growing AI inference space, cutting a licensing deal with Groq that brings both technology and experienced builders closer to Nvidia.

The agreement gives Nvidia access to Groq's inference technology, with the shared goal of making AI models run faster and at lower cost as real-world usage ramps up. As part of the move, Groq founder Jonathan Ross, President Sunny Madra, and several senior team members will join Nvidia to help scale the technology. Groq will continue operating independently, with Simon Edwards taking over as CEO.

Groq has quickly gained attention in the AI world, raising $750 million in September at a $6.9 billion valuation and targeting about $500 million in revenue this year. Ross previously helped design Google's TPU, one of the few serious alternatives to Nvidia's GPUs.

CNBC also reported Nvidia is acquiring Groq assets for roughly $20 billion, which would be its largest deal ever if confirmed. as AI moves from training to everyday use, inference is becoming the next big prize and Nvidia is moving early to secure it.

data.title: ServiceNow's $12 Billion Acquisition Spree Raises Investor Concerns
data.description:
ServiceNow (NOW, Financials) is getting a lot of attention from investors after spending more than $12 billion this year on a series of acquisitions. This is similar to the deal-driven approach that CEO Bill McDermott utilized when he was at SAP. The binge includes the $7.75 billion acquisition of cybersecurity firm Armis, as well as agreements for Moveworks and Genesys, a company that makes contact center software.

Investors fear the acquisitions will slow development, causing the company's price to decline 30% in 2025 18% before and 12% following the Armis merger. According to RBC Capital Markets analyst Matthew Hedberg, the changes may be aimed to boost revenue rather than ServiceNow's core product momentum.

McGermott called organic growth "delicious," but Guggenheim analysts now believe his strategy is "deja vu," meaning he is utilizing inorganic growth to compensate for slowdown. ServiceNow expects revenues to rise 21% to over $13 billion in 2025, similar to last year. If there are no new deals, analysts expect sales to rise by less than 20% in 2026.

Company insiders believe ServiceNow is still in a superior strategic position than SAP under McDermott and that acquisitions are not its primary expansion option.

data.title: Apple Watch Avoids Fresh Import Ban After Surprise Court Rulingimo bid for ban
data.description:
Apple can keep shipping its Apple Watch into the U.S., getting a bit of breathing room in its long-running patent fight with medical device maker Masimo.

A federal judge on Tuesday rejected Masimos request to block imports of certain Apple Watch models tied to blood-oxygen sensing technology. Judge Ana Reyes denied both a temporary restraining order and a preliminary injunction, clearing the way for Apple to continue bringing the watches into the country, according to Bloomberg Law.

The dispute centers on the Apple Watch Series 9 and Ultra 2. Masimo argues the devices infringe its patents related to light-based pulse oximetry, a claim that previously led the U.S. International Trade Commission to issue an import ban in late 2023. Apple responded by changing how blood-oxygen data is handled, shifting some processing to the paired iPhone through software updates.

Masimo says that workaround shouldnt count, and the fight isnt over. The ITC has now reopened the case to take another look.

data.title: NVDA: Cantor Fitzgerald Sees $300 Price Target for Nvidia in 2026
data.description:
Nvidia is ripe for outperformance, Cantor Fitzgerald said in a Friday note to clients.

The firm said recent weakness in AI-related names may be overstated and that Nvidia stands to benefit from a larger AI demand inflection in 2026.

Cantor Fitzgerald carries a buy-equivalent overweight rating on the chipmaker and set a $300 price target, citing durable data-center demand and product cadence.

Nvidia's shares have largely treaded water since its third-quarter results, though the stock rose about 6% over the past month as some investors returned to the market.

Analysts noted some caution around valuation and the broader technology rally, but argued those concerns may miss the longer-term secular drivers tied to AI infrastructure spending.

Cantor said catalysts such as product rollouts and data-center upgrades could lift sentiment and performance next year.

The firm's view suggests Nvidia could lead a broader market recovery should AI spending reaccelerate.

data.title: SpaceX Buys Over 1,000 Cybertrucks as Tesla Struggles With Weak Demand
data.description:
SpaceX, which is run by Elon Musk, has bought more than 1,000 Cybertrucks from Tesla (TSLA, Financials) since the automaker's electric pickup truck isn't selling as well as it used to. Electrek was the first to report on the move, which seems to be an effort to cut down on Tesla's expanding stock of Cybertrucks since sales have been underwhelming.

Tesla could make between $80 million and $160 million in internal sales from the trucks, which cost $80,000 apiece. Even while there was a lot of enthusiasm at first and more than a million preorders in 2019, just around 60,000 of those reservations have turned into real sales.

Some people say that the deal between companies shows how hard it is for Tesla to satisfy expectations for the Cybertruck, which has gotten a lot of criticism for its price, range, and design revisions since it came out. Some people also wondered whether it was right for SpaceX, which gets government contracts, to acquire cars that weren't marketed by another Musk-owned firm.

Analysts believe the move shows how Tesla's electric vehicle industry is becoming harder as competition from Chinese carmakers heats up and demand in the U.S. drops because of changing government rules.

data.title: Analyst Says Nvidia Set to Regain Leadership as AI Fears Fade
data.description:
Nvidia shares are positioned for renewed strength heading into year-end and 2026, according to analysts at Cantor Fitzgerald, who said the stock is ripe for outperformance despite recent consolidation. In a year-end note, the firm argued that investor concerns around a potential AI bubble have weighed on sentiment, but said those fears are overstated.

Cantor said Nvidia's recent sideways trading reflects caution rather than deteriorating fundamentals, noting that the broader AI trade has come under pressure even as long-term demand drivers remain intact. The firm believes the market is nearing a major AI demand inflection point and expects the valuation disconnect in Nvidia shares to correct in 2026 as enthusiasm for AI spending reaccelerates.

Cantor maintains a Buy-equivalent overweight rating and a $300 price target on the stock. Nvidia shares have gained about 3% over the past month, a muted move that analysts say reflects investors temporarily sitting on the sidelines.

data.title: Wedbush Says No Slowdown Yet for Server and Memory Demand
data.description:
Server and memory demand is still humming along, and November didn't bring any signs of a slowdown. After looking at monthly sales from several Taiwan-based suppliers, Wedbush said both server components and memory chips continued to post surprisingly strong results, helped by steady spending tied to AI and cloud infrastructure.

Wedbush analyst Matt Bryson said server component sales ran about 7% ahead of normal seasonal trends, led by suppliers serving both AI-focused and traditional servers. In other words, cloud customers are still buying across the board, not just chasing the latest AI hardware. That balance has helped keep demand resilient.

Memory trends look just as solid. Pricing strength that started earlier this year has actually picked up in Q4 as supplies tightened across DRAM and NAND. Part of that is tied to shortages in older DDR4 chips, but Bryson said demand from cloud service providers unexpectedly jumped late in the summer, making the supply crunch broader.

Wedbush now expects this strength to extend into 2026, keeping memory leaders like Micron Technology , Samsung (SSNLF), and SK hynix firmly in the spotlight.

data.title: Apple Wins Court Ruling Allowing Continued Apple Watch Imports to U.S.
data.description:
Apple (AAPL, Financials) won a court battle against Masimo when a federal judge turned down the medical technology company's attempt to stop U.S. imports of the Apple Watch. The decision lets Apple keep selling its Series 9 and Ultra 2 devices, even though Masimo says they use its blood-oxygen sensor technology without permission.

The action is based on a ruling by the International Trade Commission in 2023 that protected Masimo's patents on light-based pulse oximetry. In response, Apple released a software update that moved certain blood-oxygen computations to the iPhone that was linked with the watch. Earlier this year, U.S. Customs authorities allowed importation of the upgraded devices because of the change.

Masimo said that the Customs decision was wrong and that Apple's remedy didn't fix the alleged violation. But Judge Ana Reyes turned down Masimo's request for an injunction, noting that the firm had not completed the legal requirements to stop imports.

The ITC has started a fresh evaluation of the case, which means that there might be further decisions in the future. As of today, U.S. shops still have Apple's smartwatches in stock as we move into 2026.

data.title: Europe's Media Sector Faces Tough 2026 as AI and Ad Weakness Persist
data.description:
Bloomberg Intelligence analysts have flagged a potentially challenging 2026 for Europe's largest publishers and broadcasters as artificial intelligence disruption and a fragile macro backdrop continue to weigh on advertising demand. Based on BI data, the media and entertainment subindex of the Stoxx Europe 600 is projected to deliver earnings growth of 6.9% in 2026, trailing the broader index's expected 10% pace, after what BI describes as significant underperformance in 2025. Tom Ward, a Bloomberg Intelligence analyst, said uncertainty around advertising markets and AI disruption could persist through 2026, with global trade tensions and domestic political risks continuing to dent business confidence and marketing budgets across Europe.

Recent corporate updates underline that pressure. In October, WPP Plc cut its outlook as client demand weakened, while in November ITV Plc said widespread caution around the UK budget had hit advertising demand, prompting the broadcaster to target 35 million in cost savings. Ward estimates broadcasters' advertising sales in 2025 may have declined by mid-single digits on average, adding that visibility on when the downturn might ease remains low, given the close link between ad spending and broader economic confidence.

Alongside the macro drag, AI has emerged as a fresh source of debate for investors assessing Europe's media sector. Deutsche Bank analyst Silvia Cuneo said AI has re-entered the narrative as a potential disrupter just as tariff concerns were fading, posing risks for publishers such as Informa Plc and digital platforms including Rightmove Plc and Scout24 SE. While some analysts argue the threat may be overstated, others note that business models like higher-education digital courses at Pearson Plc could be vulnerable, and that research publishers such as Springer Nature AG & Co KGaA face additional headwinds from cuts to US research funding. For now, analysts suggest sentiment could remain subdued as investors wait to see which companies adapt quickly enough to turn AI from a risk into a possible opportunity.

data.title: US Jobless Claims Fall to 214,000 as Holiday Volatility Persists
data.description:
US unemployment data offered investors a nuanced snapshot of labor market conditions heading into year-end, with recent movements possibly reflecting seasonal dynamics rather than a fundamental shift. Initial applications for US jobless benefits fell by 10,000 to 214,000 in the week ended Dec. 20, according to Labor Department figures released Wednesday, undershooting the median Bloomberg survey estimate of 224,000. The pullback followed several weeks of volatility typical of the holiday period, after claims had jumped earlier in the month from a three-year low seen around Thanksgiving before easing again.

Beneath the headline number, signals were more mixed. Continuing claims, which track the number of people receiving unemployment benefits, rose to 1.92 million in the prior week, indicating that some displaced workers may be staying on benefits longer. The four-week moving average of initial claims also declined, a trend that helps smooth short-term distortions. However, before seasonal adjustments, initial claims increased, with New Jersey, Oregon and Washington state posting the largest gains.

Taken together, the data could still point to a labor market experiencing relatively low layoffs despite ongoing economic uncertainty. Several large employers, including PepsiCo Inc. and HP Inc. , have announced job cuts, though those announcements have not yet translated into a meaningful rise in claims. Separately, consumer confidence weakened in December for a fifth straight month, partly reflecting softer views on the labor market, with the share of consumers who say jobs are hard to get rising to its highest level since early 2021.

data.title: Snowflake in Talks for $1 Billion Observe Acquisition
data.description:
Sources have indicated that Snowflake is in discussions to acquire Observe for around $1 billion, a transaction that could become the company's largest deal to date as it continues expanding its AI and data platform footprint. The potential acquisition would move Snowflake more directly into the observability market, placing it alongside competitors such as Datadog, Dynatrace, and Splunk, which was previously acquired by Cisco. Observe, headquartered in San Mateo, California, provides monitoring and analytics tools aimed at software developers and IT teams, positioning it at the intersection of cloud operations and data-driven insight.

A notable element of the talks is that Observe already runs on Snowflake's database technology, which could reduce integration challenges if an agreement is reached. Observe's core product is an AI-powered, agentic assistant designed to investigate incidents, a capability that could complement Snowflake's existing data and AI ecosystem. For shared customers, this existing technical overlap could translate into lower friction and a more seamless experience, potentially strengthening Snowflake's value proposition in enterprise environments.

The discussions come amid a broader acquisition push by Snowflake and rival Databricks, both of which are targeting AI-focused assets as organizations increasingly experiment with autonomous, targeted AI agents built on proprietary data. Snowflake has already completed several AI-related acquisitions in 2025, including Crunchy Data, Datavolo, and TruEra AI, while its largest historical deal remains the $800 million purchase of Streamlit in March 2022. At the same time, consolidation in the observability space continues, highlighted by Palo Alto Networks' announced $3.35 billion acquisition of Chronosphere, underscoring how observability is becoming a core component of larger data, AI, and security platforms.

data.title: Federal Regulator Clears Sable Pipeline Restart, Shares Jump
data.description:
Federal pipeline regulators have approved plans that could allow Sable Offshore Corp. to restart a long-shuttered California oil pipeline, a development that helped lift the company's shares about 30% on Tuesday. The approval came after the Department of Transportation's Pipeline and Hazardous Materials and Safety Administration determined that key sections of the Las Flores Pipeline System fall under federal, rather than California, oversight. That determination could ease a major hurdle for Sable, which has been seeking to restart offshore oil production using assets acquired from Exxon Mobil Corp. last year, but has remained constrained by opposition and permitting challenges tied to the onshore pipeline network.

In a letter to the company, PHMSA approved the restart of pipeline segments CA-324 and CA-325, lines built in the 1980s that transport offshore crude once it reaches land. Line CA-324, previously known as Line 901, was responsible for the 2015 Refugio oil spill under its former operator, Plains All American, an incident that released roughly 3,000 barrels of oil along the Santa Barbara coastline. While the federal determination shifts regulatory authority, it does not alter an existing federal consent decree related to the spill, which still requires waivers from California agencies including the Office of the State Fire Marshal, according to environmental advocates.

The decision sets the stage for a renewed clash between federal and state officials over energy development along California's coast. President Donald Trump's administration has been moving to encourage domestic oil production through deregulation and proposals to open additional offshore areas to drilling, while California leaders and environmental groups have voiced strong opposition, citing ecological risks and past spill damage. Groups including the Center for Biological Diversity have indicated they are considering legal challenges, arguing that any attempt to fast-track federal permits without extended public review could conflict with state law and existing environmental safeguards.

data.title: Measles cases in South Carolina rise to 156, state health department says
data.description: The South Carolina health department reported 156 measles cases related to the ongoing outbreak in the state on Friday, with three additional cases since its last update on Tuesday.

data.title: Banks Could Start Holding XRP Due To This Simple Change
data.description:
Banks have mostly stayed on the sidelines when it comes to holding XRP directly, even as interest in digital assets continues to increase. That hesitation has not been due to a lack of utility or demand but to strict regulatory capital rules that made holding XRP economically impractical for regulated institutions.

However, a small adjustment in how XRP is treated under global banking rules could remove that barrier and change how banks interact with the cryptocurrency.

Why Banks Can’t Hold XRP

The main obstacle preventing banks from holding XRP has been its treatment under the global banking framework known as Basel III. Basel III is an international regulatory framework developed after the 2008 financial crisis that introduces higher quality and quantity of capital requirements in the international banking sector. 

Right now, XRP currently falls into the Type 2 crypto exposure under Basel III, which is set up with rules for assets that pose higher risks. Under these rules, most cryptocurrencies, including XRP, fall into a high-risk category that carries a punitive capital requirement. Banks are required to apply a 1,250% risk weight to such assets, implying they must set aside far more capital than the value of the XRP itself.

This means that under the Basel III framework, for every $1 of XRP exposure, a bank must hold $12.50 in capital. This dynamic was recently explained by a crypto commentator with the name Stern Drew on the social media platform X. 

In a post on X, Drew explained that this capital inefficiency alone accounts for years of institutional hesitation. The issue has not been demand nor technology, but the regulatory capital treatment that made holding XRP irrational from a balance sheet perspective.

The Regulatory Inflection Point

The conversation around XRP’s regulatory status is becoming increasingly important to its long-term outlook. Interestingly, Drew’s analysis goes further by pointing to what he describes as an inflection point that markets may be overlooking. Now that legal and regulatory clarity surrounding cryptocurrencies is improving, XRP could be reclassified into a lower-risk category under Basel III.

The endgame is that XRP is on a clear path to becoming a Tier-1 digital asset for global institutions, which is mostly for tokenized traditional assets and stablecoins with strong mechanisms. If that reclassification occurs, the economics will change immediately. XRP would become acceptable for direct balance sheet exposure, allowing banks to custody, deploy, and settle using the asset without the need of excessive capital. 

This is not a discussion about short-term price movements but about capital mechanics that determine whether large pools of institutional money can participate in holding XRP at all. In this case, liquidity provisioning of XRP by banks would change from off-balance-sheet usage to direct institutional ownership.

data.title: Bitcoin’s current setup looks like 2019, says Benjamin Cowen
data.description:
As Bitcoin (BTC) continues to underperform gold and major equity indices, investors are increasingly questioning whether this cycle is unfolding differently than expected. In a new interview with analyst Benjamin Cowen, we dig into why Bitcoin is lagging traditional markets, and why the current setup may feel strikingly similar to 2019.

Cowen points out that while stocks and gold are responding positively to expectations around future monetary easing, Bitcoin appears far more sensitive to actual liquidity conditions rather than optimism alone.

That distinction, he explains, helps clarify why BTC has struggled to gain momentum even as broader markets push higher. According to Cowen, Bitcoin often requires a clearer macroeconomic catalyst before it can outperform, and that catalyst may not yet be in place.

A key theme of the discussion is sentiment. Unlike previous cycle peaks characterized by widespread enthusiasm and retail speculation, this market has been marked by relative apathy.

Cowen explains why topping in an environment of low attention is unusual for Bitcoin, and how that difference could shape the path forward over the next couple of years.

The conversation also touches on the four-year cycle debate. While many commentators argue that Bitcoin’s historical cycle framework is no longer relevant, Cowen presents data suggesting that broader market cycles, not just crypto-specific narratives, still play a significant role.

He outlines why macro headwinds, including labor market trends and restrictive financial conditions, may continue to weigh on Bitcoin into 2026, even if short-term rallies occur along the way.

Rather than focusing on exact price targets, the interview centers on process over prediction; how investors can think about cycles, risk and patience in an environment where easy liquidity is not guaranteed. Cowen also briefly addresses what this means for altcoins and why expectations for quick rotations may be misplaced.

Watch the full interview on the Cointelegraph YouTube channel to hear Cowen’s complete reasoning, charts, and the deeper macro context behind his outlook.

data.title: NYMEX Overview: Petroleum Futures Moving Lower in Morning Trade — OPIS
data.description:
Petroleum futures prices were falling at midday Friday, pushed lower in light post-holiday trade as efforts to broker an end to the Russia-Ukraine war remain in the news.

Oil prices were down by about $1/bbl at 11:30 a.m. ET, with February West Texas Intermediate crude off by $1.01 to $57.34/bbl and March prices down 99cts to $57.14/bbl. Both February and March Brent crude were down by $1 to $61.24 and $60.80/bbl, respectively.

With the end of the month approaching, most activity in refined products was focused on February contracts.

February RBOB prices were 2.98cts lower to $1.7243/gal while lightly traded January futures were down 3.16cts to $1.7155/gal. February ULSD was moving 1.98cts lower to $2.1339/gal while January futures fell by 2.3cts to $2.1346/gal.

Even with the morning's losses, most petroleum futures are on track to end the shortened holiday week with gains, though any increase in selling could send ULSD to a loss.

Prices are facing headwinds Friday as Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said he plans to meet with President Trump on Sunday to discuss U.S. efforts to broker a peach between his country and Russia.

Reports indicate Zelensky seems to fell progress is being made on a U.S. proposal to end the fighting. A peace deal could lead to an easing of Western sanctions on Russian petroleum exports.

The three-day federal holiday around Christmas means that the Energy Information Administration delayed release of its weekly petroleum inventory and demand data, with data for the week ending Dec. 19 not being released until next week.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

Reporting by Steve Cronin, scronin@opisnet.com

data.description: Wall Street is underestimating CrowdStrike's potential as a "second/third derivative beneficiary of the AI Revolution," say Wedbush analysts, who maintain their $600 price target. Shares could hit $700 over the next year in a bull case scenario, with AI fueling more enterprise cybersecurity spending. data.title: Meet the BofA Investment Banker Vying to Represent Haiti in the Winter Paralympics — WSJ
data.description:
Alexander Saeedy | Photography by Nicolas Blandin for WSJ

Most investment bankers barely have time for life outside of work. Then there is Ralf Etienne, a banking associate with one leg, who is training to compete in the Winter Paralympics for his home country, Haiti.

Etienne, 36 years old, works for Bank of America in London. He spends most of his waking days in Excel and PowerPoint, building financial models and helping pitch deals to big companies and the bank's private-equity clients.

But on the weekends, he uses the limited free time he has to train with the Swiss Paralympic ski team in the Alps.

"It's a constant rotation: office, airport, mountain, back to the desk," Etienne said during an interview. "There's no comfort zone."

He has under three months to compete in international tournaments and qualify for the Winter Paralympic Games' three-track ski races, meant for athletes who compete on one leg. Haiti has already designated him as the Caribbean country's first-ever representative at the Games, which will take place this March in Italy.

If he makes it, Etienne will be one of just a few Paralympians who also work on Wall Street, where hard-charging bosses and long hours are the norm.

"Sometimes I'm leaving the office at 2 a.m. because I have work I need to finish before I get on a 6 a.m. flight to get to Switzerland," he said.

Etienne moved to London this year after leaving the U.S., where he had lived for years on a temporary work visa. The Department of Homeland Security started to put restrictions on Haitians' immigration rights in March, and Etienne worried he wouldn't be allowed to re-enter the country after traveling abroad for ski competitions.

The bank agreed to move him to London, not too far from the Alps. The hurried relocation has him behind schedule on training, but it has given him at least some peace of mind.

"The immigration process doesn't look at who I am or what I bring to the table. It just sees me as a case number," he said. "So it came down to a choice: Stay in a place that couldn't decide if it wanted me, or bet on myself, move to Europe and keep the momentum alive."

Etienne grew up outside of Port-au-Prince, the capital of Haiti. When he was 20 years old, he lost his leg during an earthquake in 2010 that killed more than 100,000 people. He was on the top floor of a four-story building in the capital that day, and both of his legs were crushed by the collapsing structure.

"I was hanging upside down for eight hours," he said. "They had to carve my flesh out of the building to get me out. Then they pushed me on a wheelbarrow to my hometown."

In the hospital, Etienne met Gregory Adamson, an American orthopedic surgeon who had traveled to Haiti to help the injured. Adamson told him to come to the U.S., where he would help Etienne get a prosthetic leg.

Etienne eventually stayed with Adamson and his family for three months in Illinois while undergoing the procedure, and they encouraged him to move to the U.S. for college. He enrolled as an undergraduate student at Bergen Community College in Paramus, N.J., in 2011, but after depleting his savings and sleeping on friends' couches for months, he felt he needed to make a change.

"I was running out of resources and realized if I don't find a better opportunity, I'm gonna have to emigrate back to Haiti," he said.

Etienne hopped on a bus and traveled from college to college to pitch himself and get a scholarship. That led him to Anderson University, a Christian university about 45 miles northeast of Indianapolis. Etienne graduated and then moved back to Haiti to work with charitable organizations. Four years later, he enrolled in business school at the University of North Carolina.

During the last year of his M.B.A. program, he caught the ski bug.

Etienne joined a group of fellow students on a ski trip to Lake Tahoe in the Sierra Nevada, but he didn't get to ski. At the resort, the instructors told him he required special lessons that had to be booked far in advance.

He vowed to find a way to make it down the slopes — except his banking career was about to start.

Etienne got a job in New York as an associate at Bank of America in 2022. He was recruited out of business school and started working in the private-equity group. When he could arrange a few days off, he would train with instructors in adaptive skiing around the U.S.

It wasn't until earlier this year that he realized he could even qualify for the Paralympics.

On a trip to Park City, Utah, Etienne met Monte Meier, a decorated paralympic skier. Meier said he was impressed by Etienne's abilities and told him he seemed to have what it takes to compete in the Games, which were coming up in about a year's time.

"He had such a strong 'I can' belief in himself that was kind of contagious," Meier said.

In the spring, Etienne took most of his paid time off and started training with a Paralympic skiing team that Meier coached. In April, he successfully completed his first competitive race in Winter Park, Colo., which made him eligible to compete in the Winter Paralympics. Haiti nominated him as the country's representative soon after.

At the same time, the Trump administration started to crack down on immigration rights for people from a number of countries, including Haiti. Companies recommended that foreigners on work visas not leave the U.S. unless absolutely necessary. Etienne worked with Bank of America to find a new job outside of the country.

"Suddenly I was spending months dealing with paperwork, relocating across continents, rebuilding my entire life from scratch," he said. "It meant losing time on the snow."

He says it was the right call. In December, the Department of Homeland Security said it would stop processing green-card applications for Haitians, which leaves people like Etienne without a path to citizenship.

Now, he is settled in his new job in London. His team at the bank is allowed to work remotely for the last two weeks of the year, which he is using to be in Switzerland to train. He plans to compete in 10 more races in Europe before the Paralympics open in March.

Etienne said that it hasn't been easy balancing his high-pressure job and training for the Paralympics. But he says he has learned how to adapt to two demanding life objectives.

"The bank didn't hire me to be a Paralympian," he said. "It actually makes me a better banker."

Write to Alexander Saeedy at alexander.saeedy@wsj.com

data.title: Tesla Stock Drops. Can It Reach $500 by 2026? — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Nate Wolf

Shares of Tesla dropped Friday, extending the stock's losing streak to three sessions to end the holiday-shortened week.

Safety concerns have dominated the conversation around Tesla stock in recent days. On Tuesday, the U.S. auto safety regulator opened an investigation into the mechanical door release on the 2022 Tesla Model 3. A petition alleged the door release was difficult to identify and use in an emergency.

The company is also nearing its self-imposed target to launch fully driverless robo-taxis, with no safety monitors, by the end of 2025. The prospect of a potentially lucrative robo-taxi business has helped Tesla stock overcome political opposition to electric vehicles this year.

Tesla shares, which are up 18% in 2025, fell 1.4% to $478.78 on Friday. The stock will need a strong run to finish the year above $500 a share after it hit an intraday high of $498.83 on Monday.

The car maker hasn't often benefited from a Santa Claus rally — the bump that U.S. stocks historically get in the last five trading days of December and first two sessions of January.

Tesla stock has declined in nine of 15 Santa Claus-rally periods as a publicly traded company, with a median drop of 2.7%, according to Dow Jones Market Data. Shares have dropped in three straight rally periods, including a 4.7% decline last year.

The S&P 500 has fallen in just four of the last 15 rally periods, per Dow Jones Market Data.

Write to Nate Wolf at nate.wolf@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: More rain expected in drenched California before drier weekend
data.description:
By Rich McKay

Storm-hit Southern California was at risk on Friday of more floods hampering millions of motorists traveling after Christmas, but the National Weather Service predicts a drier weekend.

The holiday deluge that started in earnest on Christmas Eve was spawned by the region's latest atmospheric storm, a vast airborne current of dense moisture siphoned from the Pacific, that swept inland over the greater Los Angeles area.

It dumped 6 inches of rain in the Los Angeles area with up to 18 inches of rain in the mountains, washing out some roads, and spurred evacuations and some shelter-in-place orders.

An additional 1-to-3 inches of rain is expected on Friday, said Tom Kines, a senior meteorologist with AccuWeather, a commercial forecasting company.

"Our overall picture is that there's just one more day of this mess, mostly across Southern California, specifically in the LA area," Kines said on Friday. "We still have some issues today with bouts of heavy rain, but this weekend is mainly dry, thankfully."

More than 14.5 million Californians were expected to travel by car over the Christmas holiday, according to AAA. The coming drier weather should make traveling easier, after days of slick or flooded roads, forecasters said.

The atmospheric river that brought the trouble will wind down through Friday across California with lingering heavy rainfall, heavy mountain snow, and gusty winds.

Many of the evacuation warnings issued in Los Angeles and San Bernardino counties were lifted on Christmas Day. But the orders remain on Friday in the hard-hit town of Wrightwood, a rural community with a population of about 5,000 in the San Gabriel Mountains on the border between Los Angeles and San Bernardino counties.

Aerial video footage posted online on Christmas Eve by the fire department showed rivers of mud coursing through inundated cabin neighborhoods, and mud-covered cars and homes.

Videos posted online on Thursday showed some residents scrambling over washed-out roads, picking through rubble on Christmas Day as streams of water still flowed over mounds of mud and into gullies that were once streets.

data.title: Wedbush Doubles Down on Crowdstrike as Cyber Meets AI Play Into 2026
data.description:
CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. is shaping up as one of Wedbush's top tech picks heading into 2026, as the firm highlighted the company as a leading way to play the convergence of cybersecurity and artificial intelligence. Wedbush Securities reiterated its Outperform rating and $600 price target, calling CrowdStrike a core holding on its IVES AI 30 list.

Analysts led by Dan Ives said they are seeing deal momentum broaden across both new and existing customers, supported by expanding adoption of CrowdStrike's platform. Strength has been particularly notable in Cloud, Identity, LogScale, and Data Protection, while Charlotte AI has shown rising deal flow that appears to be accelerating into the January quarter.

Wedbush noted that enterprises are still in the early innings of AI-driven cybersecurity spending, with module add-ons increasing across CrowdStrike's core verticals. The firm expects Net New ARR growth to reaccelerate in 2026 and said a bull case could see shares reach $700 over the next year.

data.title: NYMEX Overview: Petroleum Futures Moving Lower in Morning Trade — OPIS
data.description:
Petroleum futures prices were falling at midday Friday, pushed lower in light post-holiday trade as efforts to broker an end to the Russia-Ukraine war remain in the news.

Oil prices were down by about $1/bbl at 11:30 a.m. ET, with February West Texas Intermediate crude off by $1.01 to $57.34/bbl and March prices down 99cts to $57.14/bbl. Both February and March Brent crude were down by $1 to $61.24 and $60.80/bbl, respectively.

With the end of the month approaching, most activity in refined products was focused on February contracts.

February RBOB prices were 2.98cts lower to $1.7243/gal while lightly traded January futures were down 3.16cts to $1.7155/gal. February ULSD was moving 1.98cts lower to $2.1339/gal while January futures fell by 2.3cts to $2.1346/gal.

Even with the morning's losses, most petroleum futures are on track to end the shortened holiday week with gains, though any increase in selling could send ULSD to a loss.

Prices are facing headwinds Friday as Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky said he plans to meet with President Trump on Sunday to discuss U.S. efforts to broker a peach between his country and Russia.

Reports indicate Zelensky seems to fell progress is being made on a U.S. proposal to end the fighting. A peace deal could lead to an easing of Western sanctions on Russian petroleum exports.

The three-day federal holiday around Christmas means that the Energy Information Administration delayed release of its weekly petroleum inventory and demand data, with data for the week ending Dec. 19 not being released until next week.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

Reporting by Steve Cronin, scronin@opisnet.com
